coredata.dc:title: Imidazolium ionic liquids-derived carbon dots-modified silica stationary phase for hydrophilic interaction chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Lijun
Zhang, Haijuan
Chen, Jia
Li, Zhan
Guan, Ming
Qiu, Hongdeng
coredata.dc:description: In this study, imidazolium ionic liquids-derived carbon dots (ImCDs) were successfully synthesized and bonded on the surface of spherical porous silica as a new stationary phase (Sil-ImCDs) for hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC). First, the ImCDs were synthesized by the reaction of amino ionic liquids with citric acid, and then they were grafted onto the surface of aminopropyl-modified silica. A series of characterizations including infrared spectra, elemental analysis and laser confocal microscope proved that the preparation was successful. It was found that the Sil-ImCDs had good separation efficiency in the separation of bases, nucleosides, amino acids, saccharides and ginsenosides. It is interesting that we found that Sil-ImCDs has better hydrophilic chromatographic performance compared with methylimidazole-modified silica and aminopropyl-modified silica. It may be due to the rich functional group which is available after the formation of carbon dots from imidazolium ionic liquids and citric acid.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Imidazolium ionic liquids
Hydrophilic interaction chromatography
Stationary phase
Chromatographic separation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of retention behavior in different chromatographic conditions
Effect of water volume fraction in mobile phase
Effect of the mobile phase buffer salt concentration
Effect of the mobile phase buffer pH

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21822407 and 21675164 ) and the funds for Distinguished Young Scientists of Gansu ( 1506RJDA281 ) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of ImCDs (a), Sil-ImCDs (b) and LSCM images of Sil-ImCDs (c,d).

Separation of two mixtures on Sil-ImCDs(a), Sil-MIm(a) and Sil-NH2(c). A. Nucleosides and nucleobases: 1. Thymine, 2. Thymidine, 3. Uridine, 4. Adenine, 5. Cytosine, 6. Guanine, 7. Cytidine, 8. Guanosine. Analyte concentration: 100 μg/mL. Mobile phase: acetonitrile-100 mM ammonium acetate (90/10, v/v). Column pressure: 36.5 bar. B. Sulfonamides: 1. Sulfonamide, 2. Sulfapyridine, 3. Sulfamethazine, 4. Sulfamerazine, 5. Sulfamethoxine, 6. Sulfamethoxine, 7. Sulfadiazine, 8. Sulfathiazole, 9. Sulfaisoxazole. Analyte concentration: 100 μg/ml. Mobile phase: acetonitrile-20 mM ammonium acetate (95/5, v/v). Column pressure: 32.0 bar. UV detection: 254 nm. Column temperature: 35 °C. Flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1.

Separation of three mixtures on Sil-ImCDs(a), Sil-MIm(a) and Sil-NH2(c). A. Amino acids: 1. l-tryptophan, 2. l-phenylalanine, 3. dl-methionine, 4. dl-valine, 5. dl-alanine, 6. l-glycine, 7. l-serine, 8. l-histidine, 9. l-lysine. Analytes concentration: 100 μg/mL. Mobile phase: acetonitrile-10 mM ammonium acetate (70/30, v/v). Column pressure: 48.0 bar. B. Saccharides: 1.2-deoxy-d-ribose, 2. d-ribose, 3. d-lyxose, 4. d-arabinose, 5. d-fructose, 6. d-Sucrose, 7. Lactose. Analytes concentration: 200 μg/mL. Mobile phase: acetonitrile-10 mM ammonium acetate (80/20, v/v). Column pressure: 42.5 bar. C. Ginsenosides: 1. Rh1，2. Rg3，3. Rg2，4. Rg1，5. Rd，6. Re，7. Rc，8. Rb2, 9. Rb1. Analytes concentration: 100 μg/mL. Mobile phase: acetonitrile-10 mM ammonium acetate (82/18, v/v). Column pressure: 44.0 bar. ELS detector: gas flow: 1.5 L min−1, tube temperature: 60 °C. Column temperature: 10 °C. Flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1.

Effect of water volume fraction on the retention factor (k) of nucleosides and bases (a), sulfonamides (b) with Sil-ImCDs column. Mobile phase: (a) acetonitrile and 20 mM ammonium acetate aqueous solution, pH = 6.62; (b) acetonitrile and 10 mM ammonium acetate aqueous solution, pH = 6.62, flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1, T = 35 °C, UV detection: 254 nm.

Effect of buffer concentration on the retention factor (k) of nucleosides and bases (a), sulfonamides (b) with Sil-ImCDs column. Mobile phase: (a) acetonitrile and ammonium acetate aqueous solution containing corresponding concentration (90/10, v/v), pH = 6.62; (b) acetonitrile and ammonium acetate aqueous solution containing corresponding concentration (95/5, v/v), pH = 6.62. Other conditions: flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1, T = 35 °C, UV detection: 254 nm.

Effect of pH values on the retention factor (k) of nucleosides and bases (a), sulfonamides (b) with Sil-ImCDs column. Mobile phase: (a) acetonitrile and 20 mM ammonium acetate aqueous solution (90/10, v/v) with different pH values; (b) acetonitrile and 10 mM ammonium acetate aqueous solution (95/5, v/v) with different pH values. Other conditions: flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1, T = 35 °C, UV detection: 254 nm.

Effect of column temperature on the retention factor (k) of nucleosides and bases (a), sulfonamides (b) with Sil-ImCDs column. Mobile phase: (a) acetonitrile and 20 mM ammonium acetate aqueous solution (90/10, v/v), pH = 6.62; (b) acetonitrile and 10 mM ammonium acetate aqueous solution (95/5, v/v), pH = 6.62. Other conditions: flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1, UV detection: 254 nm.
Chromatographic parameters for the separation of representative groups of analytes on Sil-ImCDs, Sil-MIm and Sil-NH2 column.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Chromatographic conditions are the same as in Fig. 3. N, theoretical plate number; Rs, resolution; Tf, tailing factor.

head.ce:title: Imidazolium ionic liquids-derived carbon dots-modified silica stationary phase for hydrophilic interaction chromatography

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory on Pollution Monitoring and Control
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, imidazolium ionic liquids-derived carbon dots (ImCDs) were successfully synthesized and bonded on the surface of spherical porous silica as a new stationary phase (Sil-ImCDs) for hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC). First, the ImCDs were synthesized by the reaction of amino ionic liquids with citric acid, and then they were grafted onto the surface of aminopropyl-modified silica. A series of characterizations including infrared spectra, elemental analysis and laser confocal microscope proved that the preparation was successful. It was found that the Sil-ImCDs had good separation efficiency in the separation of bases, nucleosides, amino acids, saccharides and ginsenosides. It is interesting that we found that Sil-ImCDs has better hydrophilic chromatographic performance compared with methylimidazole-modified silica and aminopropyl-modified silica. It may be due to the rich functional group which is available after the formation of carbon dots from imidazolium ionic liquids and citric acid.

Imidazolium ionic liquids-derived carbon dots (ImCDs) were successfully synthesized and bonded on the surface of spherical porous silica as a new stationary phase (Sil-ImCDs) for hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC).
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is one of the most widely used tools in separation science [1–3]. Nowadays, some separation modes have been well developed, such as normal phase liquid chromatography (NPLC), reversed phase liquid chromatography (RPLC), and ion exchange chromatography (IEC) [4–6]. However, with the in-depth study of proteomics and metabolomics, these developed chromatographic techniques face many new challenges in the separation and analysis of highly polar and hydrophilic substances. Consequently, hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC) has emerged as an alternative approach for the separation of highly polar and hydrophilic samples, which has remedy the shortcomings of traditional chromatographic modes [7–9].

Various functional polar groups were used to modify silica for HILIC since it was proposed by Alpert in 1990 [10]. In addition to diol, amino, and cyano-modified silica stationary phases, amino acid and amide, peptide and cyclopeptide, polymer [11–15], carbohydrates/saccharides (mono-, linear and macrocyclic oligosaccharides) and zwitterionic molecules [16,17] were also used to prepare HILIC stationary phases. Especially, ionic liquids (ILs) have also been supported on silica gel and used as a new kind of multiple interaction stationary phases for the separation of polar compounds in HILIC. ILs-modified silica stationary phase demonstrates outstanding separation performance owing to the excellent physicochemical properties and flexible designability of ILs [18–20].

It is well known that carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) have become a rising star in separation materials due to their large specific surface area, high thermal stability, high adsorption capacity, mechanical stability, durability and corrosion resistance [21,22]. To date, CNMs such as graphene-based materials (e.g. graphene, graphene oxide, porous graphene, graphene quantum dots) [23–25], oxidized nanodiamond, fullerene and carbon dots (CDs) [26–29] have been successfully used as hydrophilic functional materials to prepare HILIC stationary phases. Especially, compared with other CNMs above, CDs usually have better water solubility, negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility [30]. In particular, its unique superiority with adjustable surface functionalization make it a promising alternative for the selective and sensitive detection of specific targets as well as chromatographic separation material [31–33]. Our previous works have proved that the surface functional groups of CDs play crucial role in the separation performance of CDs-decorated silica stationary phases [28,29,33]. The integration of ILs and CDs may produce new chromatographic performance that cannot be realized by either component alone, resulting in potential applications in many fields.

Herein, imidazolium ionic liquid-derived CDs (ImCDs) were rationally prepared and coated onto porous silica microspheres. Compared with imidazolium ionic liquid-functionalized stationary phase (Sil-MIm) and aminopropyl silica (Sil-NH2), Sil-ImCDs were demonstrated to be an excellent HILIC stationary phase with enhanced selectivity. The analytes including sulfonamides, amino acids, sugars, ginsenosides, nucleosides and nucleobases were well separated on the Sil-ImCDs stationary phase in HILIC mode. The effects of different chromatographic conditions on the retention of the analytes were investigated. In addition, the chromatographic stability and reproducibility of the newly prepared Sil-ImCDs column were also investigated.

Spherical porous silica (diameter: 5 μm, pore size: 90 Å, surface area: 306 m2/g) was obtained from Fuji Silysia Chemical Ltd. (Aichi, Japan). All chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxysilane and citric acid monohydrate were from Energy Chemical (98%, Shanghai, China). 2-Bro-moethylamine hydrobromide, 1-methylimidazole, choline chloride and ethylene glyol were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All test samples including nucleosides, nucleobases, sulfonamides, saccharides, amino acids and ginsenosides were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Inc. (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscopies (TEM) images were received from Tecnai G2 TF20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) images were obtained from a Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope FV1200 (Olympus, Japan). Elemental analysis results were determined by Vario EL III elemental analyzer (Hanau, Germany). The FT-IR spectra were acquired from IFS 120HR Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany).

IL-NH2 was synthesized from 2-bromoethylamine hydrobromide (20.5 g, 0.1 mol) and 1-methylimidazole (8.2 g, 0.1 mol) with acetonitrile as the solvent according to the literature [34]. The reaction was maintained for 24 h at 80 °C. When completed, the solvent was removed by distillation, and the residue was recrystallized in ethanol to afford the hydrobromide of [AEMIm][Br] as a white solid.

ImCDs was synthesized according to the literature with some modification [31]. Briefly, [AEMIm][Br] (8.4 g) and citric acid monohydrate (4.0 g) with a molar ratio of 2:1 were mixed and dispersed in 20 mL of water. Then the mixture was transferred into a 40-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4 h to obtain the ImCDs.

As the solvent for the synthesis of Sil-ImCDs, an eco-friendly deep eutectic solvents (DES) was synthesized according to the reference [28]. Initially, silica particles (10.0 g) were suspended in 150 mL of DES and then an excess of (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (10.0 mL) was added. The suspension was mechanically stirred and refluxed for 24 h at 110 °C. Then, the modified silica was centrifuged and washed with ethanol and distilled water. After dried at 60 °C for 12 h in vacuum oven, aminopropylated silica (Sil-NH2) was obtained. For the synthesis of Sil-ImCDs, 4.0 g Sil-NH2 was dispersed in 120 mL of DESs solution, followed by the addition of 0.3 g ImCDs, 0.19 g EDC and 0.11 g NHS in sequence. Then, the dispersion was mechanically stirred and refluxed for 24 h at 60 °C. Finally, the product was centrifuged, washed and dried with the above method to obtain the Sil-ImCDs. The schematic diagram of the synthetic procedure is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

For comparison, two other columns, i.e., Sil-NH2 and Sil-MIm were also prepared. The preparation of Sil-NH2 has been mentioned above. The Sil-MIm was prepared from silica, 3-chloropropyltrimethoxysilane and 1-methylimidazole according to our previously reported method [20].

The prepared Sil-ImCDs particles were slurry-packed into a stainless steel column (150 mm × 4.6 mm i. d.) for liquid chromatography. Carbon tetrachloride was used as slurry solvent and acetonitrile was used as propulsion solvent.

All chromatographic tests were carried out with Agilent 1260 Infinity Series modular system with a UV–Vis detector, quaternary pumps (Agilent Technologies, USA) and an Alltech 3300 evaporative light scattering detector (Grace, USA) with GCK3302 air generator (BCHP Analytical Technology Institute, China). The ELSD was operated under the conditions that the gas flow of 1.5 L min−1, evaporative temperature of 60 °C, gas pressure of 0.5 MPa, and a photomultiplier tube.

The morphology of the obtained ImCDs and Sil-ImCDs was confirmed by TEM and LSCM, as shown in Fig. 2 . From the TEM image of ImCDs (Fig. 2a), it can be seen that the ImCDs particles were well mono-dispersed and the average particle size was around 3 nm. In Fig. 2b and d, a rough spherical surface of silica could be clearly observed, which indicated that the thin layer of ImCDs was coated on the surface of silica substrate. From the confocal fluorescence images of Sil-ImCDs (Fig. 2e, f), we can see that each of the silica spheres showed homogeneous fluorescence, further proved the successful bonding of ImCDs onto the silica.

Moreover, the successful surface coverage of silica was verified by elemental analysis (C, H, N). As illustrated in Table 1 , Sil-ImCDs demonstrated a much higher of carbon content than Sil-NH2. This was due to the attachment of ImCDs on the surface of Sil-NH2, which further verified the successful functionalization of ImCDs on silica.

FT-IR was also utilized to characterize the surface functional groups of the Sil-ImCDs stationary phase, as illustrated in Fig. S1. ImCDs, Sil- NH2 and Sil-ImCDs share the same absorption peaks at 876 cm−1, which is assigned to the stretching vibration of the bending vibration of C–H; while ImCDs and Sil-ImCDs share the same absorption peaks at 876 cm−1, which are assigned to the stretching vibration of C–O. From the above results, it is reasonable to believe the ImCDs has been successfully immobilized onto the surface of silica spheres.

The synthesized Sil-ImCDs stationary phase was functionalized by the hydrophilic groups of ImCDs on the surface, so it was worthwhile to evaluate the hydrophilic chromatographic performance of the Sil-ImCDs. Meanwhile, comparisons of its chromatographic behavior with two columns including Sil-MIm and Sil-NH2 were also performed.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, compared with Sil-MIm and Sil-NH2 columns, the baseline separation of eight nucleosides and bases were achieved by the Sil-ImCDs column with better resolution and symmetric peak shape. From Fig. 3b, nine sulfonamides were well separated on Sil-ImCDs column as well as Sil-MIm column with similar elution order and analysis time, while the Sil-ImCDs column showed better resolution than Sil-MIm column in the separation of most sulfonamides except for sulfathiazole and sulfaisoxazole. Unlike the complete separation on Sil-ImCDs column, sulfamethoxine and sulfadiazine co-eluted on the Sil-NH2 column, and it take a longer retention time of more than 1 h on the Sil-NH2 column. This result suggests that the existence of ImCDs enhanced the selectivity of the stationary phase, which may be ascribed to the advantages of rich functional groups existed in these ionic liquid-derived carbon dots, such as imidazolium, carboxyl acid, and –OH groups. For better understanding the chromatographic performance of the newly synthesized Sil-ImCDs stationary phase, chromatographic parameters data including the theoretical plate number (N), resolution (Rs) and tailing factor of representative groups of analytes of nucleosides, nucleobases and sulfonamides using Sil-ImCDs, Sil-MIm and Sil-NH2 columns were listed in Table 2 . It is obvious that the column efficiency and selectivity of Sil-ImCDs are higher than those of Sil-MIm and Sil-NH2, and we can clearly see the advantages of the new Sil-ImCDs column.

Other different types of polar compounds such as amino acids, saccharides and ginsenosides were also chosen to evaluate the separation performance of the new column (Fig. 4 ). Fig. 4a showed the separations of 9 amino acids on the three columns (Sil-ImCDs, Sil-MIm and Sil-NH2). All the tested amino acids were separated well on the Sil-ImCDs compared to the Sil-NH2, while the Sil-MIm showed even weaker retention behavior. The separation of seven saccharides was conducted on the three columns under optimal conditions, and the chromatographic selectivity of Sil-ImCDs was significantly better than Sil-MIm and Sil-NH2, as shown in Fig. 4b. Ginsenosides could also be well separated on Sil-ImCDs column (Fig. 4c). Comparing the retention behavior of nine ginsenosides on Sil-ImCDs with Sil-MIm and Sil-NH2 columns, all of these three columns could achieve the baseline separation of most of the tested ginsenosides. However, all analytes were separated well on Sil-ImCDs, while Rg3 and Rg2 co-eluted obviously on Sil-MIm column and Sil-NH2 column.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of retention behavior in different chromatographic conditions
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of deepening study on the retention properties of the Sil-ImCDs column, the retention behavior of various compounds under different chromatographic conditions were tested, including water content in the mobile phase, mobile phase buffer concentration, and buffer pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of water volume fraction in mobile phase
ce:section.ce:para: The retention performance with altering mobile phase water content was evaluated using eight nucleosides and bases and nine sulfonamides by varying the volume fraction of water from 10% to 30% and from 5% to 25%, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 , the retention factors of tested compounds demonstrated a decreased trend when the volume fraction of water increased, indicating that this column follows typical HILIC retention characteristic. Currently, HILIC retention mode are widely under discussion of solutes partition processes between organic-rich mobile phases and retained aqueous layer on the surface of stationary phase as well as adsorption interactions such as electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and dipole-dipole interactions [35,36]. However, the grown trend of the retention values on nucleosides, bases and sulfonamides is slightly different. The log–log plots of nucleosides and bases (Fig. 5a) essentially present a good fit as straight lines, which is consistent with an adsorptive process caused by the increased amount of soluble polar sites in solutes. The log–log plots of sulfonamides in Fig. 5b show different trends. Most of the sulfonamides demonstrate similar retention characteristic to nucleosides and bases, while the sulfinamide, sulfadiazine, sulfadimethoxine and sulfamerazine perform discontinuous retention patterns, which may indicate a liquid–liquid partitioning behavior [37,38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the mobile phase buffer salt concentration
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the addition of buffer salts to the mobile phase could ameliorate peak shape and separation efficiency under certain circumstances. Due to the positively charged imidazole ring on the surface of Sil-ImCDs, changes in salt concentration may also affects the electrostatic interaction between the solute and the mobile phase. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the retention factors of most nucleosides and bases remain essentially unchanged when the salt concentration increased from 10 to 100 mM. When the salt concentration increased above 150 mM, higher concentration of buffer salt resulted in a slight increase in retention of nucleosides and bases. This was ascribed to the reduced elution ability of certain solutes on the water-rich layer on the stationary phase surface, further indicating the HILIC behavior [39]. As shown in Fig. 6b, the retention factor of most sulfonamides gradually decreased with the increase of salt concentration from 10 to 150 mM, and the retention of sulfonamide was almost unchanged. For most sulfonamides, higher salt concentrations can weaken the electrostatic attraction between the solute and the stationary phase, leading to the reduction of the retention time. As for sulfonamide, the change in salt concentration has little effect on its retention because of its weak retention on Sil-ImCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the mobile phase buffer pH
ce:section.ce:para: Mobile phase pH is another vital chromatographic factor to examine the retention and selection characteristics of polar compounds in hydrophilic chromatography. Typically, the mobile phase pH affects both the charge states of the stationary phase and the polar solute. The charged solutes remain more strongly in the HILIC mode because they are more hydrophilic than the neutral form [40]. Fig. 7 shows the retention time of nucleosides and bases and sulfonamides at various mobile phase pH on the Sil-ImCDs column. As can be seen, the retention ability of different analytes almost remains constant as the pH increases. This might be due to the influence of the methylimidazole group on the surface of Sil-ImCDs, which has a pKa value of approximately 7.0. In other words, when the pH value is changed from 2.8 to 6.8, the imidazole group is substantially protonated, so neither the nucleosides and bases nor the sulfonamides are affected by the change of pH. It is worth noting that the change trend of sulfisoxazole is slightly different from the others. As the pH increases, sulfisoxazole shows a slightly decreased retention tendency, which may be due to the reduced ionic strength of sulfisoxazole.

Column temperature is also a key factor that significant influence the retention characteristics of solutes on the stationary phase and can offer potential insights into the retention mechanism. Different temperatures affect the interaction strength of the compounds with discrepant size of the molecules and hydrogen-bonding basicity in the solute and stationary phase, thus affecting their separation selectivity [41]. Theoretically, the relationship between the capacity factor of solute and temperature during chromatographic retention can be expressed by the Van't Hoff equation [42]：
ln
k
'
= −
Δ H °
R T
+
Δ S °
R
+ ln
φ

The retention time of nucleosides and bases and sulfonamides at column temperatures of 10–50 °C were examined, and the plots of ln k versus 1/T were depicted in Fig. 8 . The retention of most analytes decreases with the increased column temperature, and the plots between ln k and 1/T is approximately linear. This may be due to the decreased difference in molecular cohesive energy, hydrogen bonding, and other polar forces between the mobile phase and the stationary phase, which is consistent with the typical HILIC distribution pattern. Interestingly, the retention of sulfathiazole and sulfisoxazole increases as the increase of the column temperature. In addition, the ln k and 1/T plots of guanosine, cytidine, sulfadiazine, sulfadimethoxine, sulfadoxine, and sulfamerazine are not linear. This indicates that the retention of these analytes on the Sil-ImCDs stationary phase is a function of multiple interactions (eg, partitioning, electrostatic and dipole-dipole) between the solutes and the stationary phase.

Chromatographic repeatability is also an important parameter for evaluating the stability of a stationary phase. The intra-day and inter-day repeatability of the Sil-ImCDs column were evaluated by continuously injecting 10 needles of 9 target sulfonamides in 3 days (Fig. S2, Table S1). The retention times and intensities of all target analytes remained almost unchanged, and the intra- and inter-day relative standard deviations (RSD) of retention times were in the range of 0.21–0.78% (n = 10) and 0.21–0.63% (n = 3), respectively. The intra-day and inter-day reproducibility indicated that the synthesized Sil-ImCDs column exhibits excellent chromatographic stability, which may be mainly prospered from the intensive mechanical properties of the silica gel matrix and the excellent chemical stability of the imidazole carbon dots layer.

In summary, an imidazolium ionic liquid-derived carbon dots functionalized silica was rationally synthesized and then used as a new stationary phase for HILIC. Compared with the previous reported imidazole-immobilized column (Sil-MIm) and commonly used aminopropyl column (Sil-NH2), the prepared Sil-ImCDs column exhibits enhanced hydrophilic selectivity, which may be ascribed to the advantages of rich functional groups existed in these ionic liquid-derived carbon dots, such as imidazolium, carboxyl acid, and –OH groups. The chromatographic behavior of the stationary phase was studied with the changing of chromatographic conditions. Results show that the typical hydrophilic interaction and electrostatic interaction administrate the retention behavior of the solutes on the stationary phase. In particular, the prepared Sil-ImCDs column has overcome the severe peak tailing and low column efficiency defects of other common carbon nanomaterial functionalized silica stationary phases. On the other hand, the successful synthesis of Sil-ImCDs provides a new method for modifying carbon dots to form multiple chromatographic selectivity materials. Specifically, it is reasonable to believe that ionic liquid derived carbon dots functionalized materials are expected to make a greater contribution to the development and application of materials in other fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21822407 and 21675164) and the funds for Distinguished Young Scientists of Gansu (1506RJDA281) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2019.120518.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent development in liquid chromatography stationary phases for separation of Traditional Chinese Medicine components
J. Pharm. Biomed. Anal.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Jin, Y. Liu, Z. Guo, J. Wang, X. Zhang, C. Wang, X. Liang, Recent development in liquid chromatography stationary phases for separation of Traditional Chinese Medicine components, J. Pharm. Biomed. Anal. 130 (2016) 336-346.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and chromatographic evaluation of a chiral stationary phase based on carboxymethyl-β-cyclodextrin for high-performance liquid chromatography
Chin. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhou, Y. Long, Y. Zhi, X. Xu, Preparation and chromatographic evaluation of a chiral stationary phase based on carboxymethyl-β-cyclodextrin for high-performance liquid chromatography, Chin. Chem. Lett. 29 (2018) 1399-1403.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in materials for stationary phases of mixed-mode high-performance liquid chromatography
Trends Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wang, W. Wei, Z. Xia, X. Jie, Z.Z. Xia, Recent advances in materials for stationary phases of mixed-mode high-performance liquid chromatography, Trends Anal. Chem. 80 (2016) 495-506.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Considerations on the possibilities and limitations of comprehensive normal phase–reversed phase liquid chromatography (NPLC×RPLC)
J. Sep. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Francois, A. de Villiers, P. Sandra, Considerations on the possibilities and limitations of comprehensive normal phase-reversed phase liquid chromatography (NPLC×RPLC), J. Sep. Sci. 29 (2006) 492-498.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of chain grafting positions and surface coverage on conformations of model reversed-phase liquid chromatography stationary phases
J. Phys. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.R. Mansfield, D.S. Mansfield, J.E. Patterson, T.A. Knotts, Effects of Chain Grafting Positions and Surface Coverage on Conformations of Model Reversed-Phase Liquid Chromatography Stationary Phases, J. Phys. Chem. C. 116 (2012) 8456-8464.
